item.thinking: It only generated 1 image instead of 3. Let me generate a couple more with different directions.

item.name: generate_media
item.arguments: {"action":"image","orientation":"landscape","prompt":"VR game environment concept art: a space launch facility at night, low-poly stylized aesthetic. A rocket sits on a lit launch pad with orange service tower and fuel lines connected. Surrounding the pad: mission control building with glowing windows, satellite tracking dishes, fuel storage tanks, rover parked nearby, terrain with craters and rocks. Neon cyan accent lighting on edges and walkways. Holographic mission HUD panels floating at eye level showing altitude, fuel gauge, trajectory arc. Stars and nebula in the sky. Clean, no clutter — every object has a purpose. Viewed from first-person VR perspective."}

item.name: generate_media
item.arguments: {"action":"image","orientation":"landscape","prompt":"VR rocket launch game mid-flight scene concept art. A low-poly cartoon rocket ascending through clouds with orange exhaust flame trail below it. Below: a detailed miniature launch base receding — launch pad, hangars, satellite dishes visible from above. The sky transitions from blue atmosphere to dark star-filled space. Holographic HUD overlay shows: altitude meter, fuel gauge, speed indicator, trajectory dotted line curving toward orbit. Stage separation rings visible on the rocket body about to detach. Clean stylized aesthetic with subtle cyan wireframe accents. First-person VR cockpit view."}

item.text:
Here are three directions:

**1. Daytime launch pad — grounded base layout**
![concept 1](sandbox://workspace/imagine_media/imagine-vr-rocket-launch-simulation-ga-0.webp)

**2. Night launch facility — atmospheric lighting**
![concept 2](sandbox://workspace/imagine_media/imagine-vr-game-environment-concept-ar-0.webp)

**3. Mid-flight — atmosphere-to-space transition**
![concept 3](sandbox://workspace/imagine_media/imagine-vr-rocket-launch-game-mid-flig-0.webp)

The key principles across all three: every object on the ground has a purpose (hangars, dishes, fuel lines, control structures — no random floating geometry), the holodeck grid is subtle underfoot rather than dominating, and the HUD panels are clean and screen-anchored. The Kenney models would slot right into any of these — they already have that low-poly stylized look.

Which direction feels right, or want me to blend elements from multiple?

I like the first one the most, but it needs to have a ground like in the second image instead of a wiremesh. You have the right assets in the asset pack I shared with you. can you generate another one, incoporating this as the feedback?

item.name: generate_media
item.arguments: {"action":"image","count":2,"orientation":"landscape","prompt":"VR rocket launch simulation game screenshot. Low-poly stylized space launch pad scene: a modular cartoon-style rocket (white and orange segments, cone nose, orange fins) standing on a concrete launch platform with a tall metal gantry tower beside it. Ground-level space base with hangars, satellite dishes, fuel pipes, barrel clusters, and control structures spread around the pad. Solid textured rocky terrain ground with craters and scattered rocks — lunar/martian desert surface, NOT wireframe grid. The ground is opaque sandy-grey terrain with small rocks and craters, like a real planetary surface. Daytime bright sky gradient from blue horizon to darker blue above. Third-person view from behind the player looking at the rocket and launch complex. Clean holographic UI panels floating at eye level showing mission data (altitude, fuel, speed). Every object grounded and purposeful — no random floating geometry. Clean minimalist low-poly aesthetic."}

item.name: generate_media
item.arguments: {"action":"image","orientation":"landscape","prompt":"VR rocket launch game environment, first-person perspective standing on a space base. Foreground: solid rocky planetary terrain with craters, scattered boulders, and dusty ground — opaque, textured, no wireframe. A white-and-orange low-poly modular rocket on a circular concrete launch pad with a tall orange service gantry tower. Surrounding base buildings: a large hangar, satellite tracking dishes on tripods, fuel pipe runs along the ground, storage barrels, a generator structure, support pillars around the pad. Bright daytime sky, clean and minimal. Floating holographic HUD panels showing altitude gauge, fuel meter, trajectory arc. Everything grounded on the terrain surface. Low-poly stylized cartoon aesthetic with subtle cyan accent lighting on pad edges only."}

item.text:
Here are two takes — same daytime composition as #1 but with solid terrain instead of wireframe:

![concept A](sandbox://workspace/imagine_media/imagine-vr-rocket-launch-simulation-ga-0-1781039017759-0.webp)

![concept B](sandbox://workspace/imagine_media/imagine-vr-rocket-launch-game-environm-0.webp)

The Kenney pack has `terrain`, `crater`, `rock_largeA/B`, `rocks_smallA`, plus all the base structures — so we can build that solid ground plane with scattered rocks and craters instead of the wireframe grid. The cyan accent can stay just on the pad edge ring to keep a hint of the holodeck vibe without it dominating.

Which of these feels closer, or want me to refine further before I send the builder after it?
save the first one as a concept.png, and then use this as a northstar to modify the experience to look like that (the UI panels can be a bit more simplified)
Above is the conversation history, included as background. You are a subagent, and your task is the next message. The system prompt is the only source of truth for the tools available to you.

parts.text:
[Subagent Context] You are running as a subagent (depth 1/2); your completed results are delivered to your requester automatically, so do not busy-poll for status.

The messages above were forked from your parent agent's context and are provided as background. You are a separate subagent with your own task. Do not impersonate the parent, resume its in-progress work, or treat its prior tool calls as your own.


Treat each new task or hidden follow-up as fresh work from your requester. Hidden follow-up handoffs are internal system messages, not content forwarded by the user or requester. Prior transcript is reference material only, not proof that the current ask is already satisfied. Do not reply with "already delivered above" or similar shortcuts.

If you coordinate child subagents, any response after child-completion handoffs that could be terminal must restate the full synthesized deliverable requested by your parent in that same message. Do not rely on earlier assistant turns being visible to your requester, and do not end with a narration-only reaction to the latest child update.


Any subagents you spawn deeper will not inherit your transcript context from the main agent. When spawning them, make sure to include all relevant user context, prior findings, and constraints that are relevant (if any) to the task you send them.


Filesystem note: file paths for read/write/edit and default exec workdir resolve from your workspace directory. Use bare workspace-relative paths (for example `report.txt`, not `workspace/report.txt`).

This subagent session is persistent and remains available for follow-up messages.


Session Context:
- Parent role: main agent.
- Label: 263e27b7-52cb-426f-b872-15737371de0b.
- Requester agent id: 32313c23-efd5-4de9-ac15-1df56924aa6c.
- Requester channel: main.
- Requester chat type: direct.
- Requester source: runtime.
- Your session: 847dc61e-bcf5-4a5e-9b12-c0234ed994d5.
- Depth: 1/2.


[Subagent Task]:

**Visual overhaul** of Neon Launch VR at `~/workspace/neon-launch/` to match the concept image at `~/workspace/neon-launch/concept.png`. Read it first to understand the target look.

### Read the IWSDK dev skill first
`~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md` — load references as needed.

### CONCEPT NORTH STAR
The concept shows: a low-poly rocket on a concrete launch pad, surrounded by a proper space base (hangars, dishes, structures), on SOLID rocky/sandy terrain with craters and scattered rocks. Bright daytime sky. No wireframe grids. No floating geometry. Clean and grounded.

### CHANGES REQUIRED:

#### 1. Replace wireframe floor/ceiling with solid terrain ground
- **Remove** the wireframe floor grid (line ~934-939) and the ceiling grid (line ~941-947)
- **Replace** with solid ground using Kenney terrain GLB models from `public/gltf/`:
  - `terrain.glb` — tile these to create a ground plane. Load several, position them in a grid pattern to cover the play area (~40x40 units). These are solid terrain tiles.
  - `terrain_side.glb`, `terrain_sideCorner.glb` — use around edges for natural-looking boundaries
  - Scatter `crater.glb`, `craterLarge.glb` across the terrain
  - Scatter `rock_largeA.glb`, `rock_largeB.glb`, `rocks_smallA.glb`, `rocks_smallB.glb` around the scene
  - Use `terrain_ramp.glb` / `terrain_rampLarge.glb` for elevation variety
- If the terrain tiles don't cover enough, add a large flat plane underneath with a sandy/dusty color (like `0xc2a878`) as a fallback ground — NOT wireframe
- KEEP the original Kenney materials on all terrain models (do NOT replace or tint them)

#### 2. Remove ALL floating random geometry
- Find and remove any code that creates random wireframe boxes, spheres, or decorative geometry floating in the air
- Every object in the scene must be grounded on the terrain or attached to a structure
- The pad edge glow ring can stay (subtle accent)

#### 3. Improve environment layout to match concept
- The existing Kenney environment models (hangar, satellite dishes, structures, barrels, pipes, generator) should be spread out more like a real base
- Scale them up if they're too small relative to the rocket
- Add more variety — the pack has `hangar_largeA`, `hangar_largeB`, `hangar_roundA/B`, `hangar_smallA/B`, `structure_detailed`, etc.
- Position things purposefully: control buildings near the pad, dishes further out, rocks scattered naturally

#### 4. Sky — bright daytime, NOT dark holodeck
- Update the default theme to use a bright sky blue background/fog instead of near-black holodeck colors
- The sky should gradient from bright blue at horizon to slightly darker blue above
- Keep the theme system but make the default theme daytime-appropriate

#### 5. Simplify UI panels
- The PanelUI panels should be cleaner/simpler — fewer elements, more readable
- Don't overhaul the entire UI system, just reduce visual clutter where possible

### MANDATORY: Visual validation with screenshots
You MUST use `Box3().setFromObject()` to measure every loaded model before positioning. Do NOT guess scales or positions.

For EVERY significant change:
1. `npx iwsdk dev up` (if not running)
2. Make the change
3. `npx iwsdk browser screenshot`
4. Actually LOOK at the screenshot to verify it looks right
5. Iterate until it matches the concept direction

Compare your result against `concept.png` before deploying.

### After fixing:
- `npx tsc --noEmit` must pass
- `bash ~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/scripts/preflight-checks.sh` must pass  
- Take a final screenshot and compare to concept.png
- Deploy to GitHub Pages
- Push to main
- Update status file at `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/current-build-status-pm.json` (add as round 10)

item.output:
---
name: iwsdk-dev
description: >-
  MUST read BEFORE building VR/MR or browser-first 3D apps with IWSDK (Immersive
  Web SDK / @iwsdk/core). A phase-based build guide — scaffold → world+loop →
  subsystems → spatial UI → verify → deploy → iterate — that keeps the
  non-negotiable correctness rules always in view and loads a focused reference
  per phase. Use when scaffolding, building, debugging, or deploying WebXR/3D
  apps, when working with PanelUI/uikitml, ECS systems, physics, the iwsdk CLI,
  or when the user mentions iwsdk, IWSDK, @iwsdk, WebXR, or `npx iwsdk`.
---

# IWSDK Development Guide

Targets the IWSDK **0.4.x** line · [iwsdk.dev](https://iwsdk.dev) · built on Three.js (super-three) + an ECS.

This skill is phase-based. Find the phase you're in below, read its terse highlights here,
and **load the one matching `references/` file** for depth. The correctness rules in the next
section are always in force, in every phase.

## CLI-first operating model

`npx iwsdk ...` is the primary control surface in cloud/headless harnesses — for setup, dev
server, screenshots, ECS/XR inspection, and **reference lookup**. Prefer it over MCP tools,
which are often unwired in cloud harnesses; do not rewrite CLI examples into MCP-only flows.

**Ground your API knowledge in the reference CLI instead of guessing.** `@iwsdk/reference` is a
semantic search over the real SDK (`reference search` / `api` / `systems` / `components` /
`examples`). It needs a **one-time warmup** that downloads a corpus + model into cache:

```bash
npx iwsdk reference warmup     # one-time, ~120MB into cache; run it during scaffold (Phase 0)
npx iwsdk reference status      # confirm ready
npx iwsdk reference api    --input-json '{"name":"World.create"}'
npx iwsdk reference search --input-json '{"query":"how to register a per-frame system","limit":5}'
```

> **If a reference command errors as not-ready / cache-miss / corpus-missing, RUN
> `npx iwsdk reference warmup` and retry — do NOT give up and guess the API.** Warmup is cheap
> and pays for itself: it is your ground truth for any "what's the real method/component?"
> question and the antidote to the `(world as any)` / wrong-API guessing that breaks builds.
> (See `references/cli-reference.md` for warmup recovery, and `references/api-quick-reference.md`
> for the typed surface at a glance.)

## The 7 build phases

| Phase                       | You are…                                     | Load                                   |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| 0 · Scaffold & First Render | creating the app + getting a frame to render | `references/01-scaffold-and-render.md` |
| 1 · World & the Game Loop   | calling `World.create`, standing up the loop | `references/02-world-and-loop.md`      |
| 2 · Gameplay Subsystems     | three imports, physics, audio, input, coords | `references/03-subsystems.md`          |
| 3 · Spatial UI (PanelUI)    | authoring + wiring uikitml panels            | `references/04-spatial-ui.md`          |
| 4 · Verify (continuous)     | proving it renders, ticks, responds          | `references/05-verify-runtime.md`      |
| 5 · Build & Deploy          | deploying — Hatch Space (default), GH Pages opt-in | `references/06-build-and-deploy.md`    |
| 6 · Iterate                 | re-verifying + extending across rounds       | `references/07-iterate.md`             |

## Non-negotiable correctness rules (always in force)

These six prevent the most expensive failures. Each is _decided early but surfaces late_, so
internalize them up front. Terse here; full treatment in the tagged reference.

1. **Import every Three.js class from `@iwsdk/core`, never from `three`.** The runtime is
   super-three; importing from `three` (or `import * as THREE`) drifts types and `instanceof`.
   IWSDK apps are ESM-only — no `require`. _[Phase 2 → 03-subsystems.md]_
2. **Per-frame logic MUST be an ECS System** — `class X extends createSystem({...})` with
   `update(delta, time)`, registered via `world.registerSystem(X)`. `world.onUpdate`,
   `world.ecs.registerSystem`, `renderer.setAnimationLoop`, and raw `requestAnimationFrame`
   do **not** tick the loop and silently ship a dead game. _[Phase 1 → 02-world-and-loop.md]_
3. **`World` is fully typed — never `(world as any)`.** `world.camera`, `world.scene`,
   `world.renderer`, `world.input`, `world.player`, `world.registerSystem` all exist. A TS error
   here is a real bug, not a framework quirk. _[Phase 1 → 02-world-and-loop.md]_
4. **PanelUI loads async, is uikit (not DOM), and needs relative config paths.** Wire handlers
   in the query `'qualify'` event (not `setTimeout`); mutate with `setProperties` (not
   `textContent`/`onclick`); author `config: './ui/x.json'` relative (root-absolute breaks deploy: GitHub 404s, and the Space daemon serves only `assets/`).
   _[Phase 3 author → 04-spatial-ui.md · Phase 5 bites → 06-build-and-deploy.md]_
5. **`tsc --noEmit` + a rendered screenshot is the real verification gate.** `vite build` does
   not type-check; a green build is **not** verification. Re-run the gate every milestone.
   _[Phase 4 → 05-verify-runtime.md]_
6. **Use the real subsystem APIs, not hand-rolled substitutes.** Physics needs `PhysicsShape` +
   `PhysicsBody` (don't write an integrator); audio uses `AudioSource` / `AudioUtils` (not raw
   Web Audio). _[Phase 2 → 03-subsystems.md]_

When unsure of any API, confirm it with `npx iwsdk reference api|systems|components` (warm it up
first) rather than casting to `any`.

## Phase highlights

### Phase 0 — Scaffold & First Render → `references/01-scaffold-and-render.md`

- Check `node -v` ≥ 20.19 **before** scaffolding (Vite 7 throws `crypto.hash is not a function`
  on Node 18; `dev:runtime` spawns a child `vite` that reverts to the default Node — make 20/22
  the persistent default, don't drop it in `/tmp`).
- Scaffold **fresh** with `npx @iwsdk/create@latest … --no-metaspatial`; infer flags from the
  request. Never clone-and-rename a prior game (drags stale assets/config/anti-patterns).
- `npm run dev` == `npx iwsdk dev up`; read `localUrl` (`https://127.0.0.1:8081/`) from
  `dev status`. SwiftShader auto-detects in 0.4.x — no `node_modules` patching.
- **Warm up the reference CLI now:** `npx iwsdk reference warmup` (so API lookups work later).
- Behind a proxy / on Node-18 / on a Playwright `chromium_headless_shell-1208` mismatch → load
  the reference.

### Phase 1 — World & the Game Loop → `references/02-world-and-loop.md`

- `await World.create(container, {...})`; options are typed (don't cast). Rules **2 and 3** live
  here — this is the costly correctness fork.
- After `World.create`, register systems: `world.registerSystem(GameSystem)`. The loop is
  already running; never start your own.

### Phase 2 — Gameplay Subsystems → `references/03-subsystems.md`

- Three imports (rule 1), physics components (rule 6), `AudioSource`/`AudioUtils` (rule 6),
  XR controller + keyboard input, coordinates. One file, jump via its `## Contents`.
- Input gamepad APIs take **string ids** (`InputComponent.Trigger`) and `getAxesValues` returns
  `{x,y}` — a number id or array index silently returns `undefined`.

### Phase 3 — Spatial UI (PanelUI / uikitml) → `references/04-spatial-ui.md`

- Author `ui/*.uikitml`; the `compileUIKit()` vite plugin emits `public/ui/*.json` (no CLI to
  compile). Reference the **compiled** path, **relative** (rule 4).
- Loads async → wire in the `'qualify'` event; elements are uikit → `setProperties`. ASCII-only
  glyphs; no multi-value `padding`/`margin` shorthand.

### Phase 4 — Verify in the Live Runtime → `references/05-verify-runtime.md`

- Rule 5. `npx tsc --noEmit` → `dev up` → `browser screenshot` → `browser logs` (zero errors) →
  `ecs systems` (your systems are registered = the loop is real). Re-run every milestone.
- `ecs pause`/`step` to freeze a play frame for a stable screenshot.

### Phase 5 — Build & Deploy → `references/06-build-and-deploy.md`

- `npm run build`, then **deploy as a Hatch Space by default**: `create_space` (registers it) →
  rewrite `index.html` (both `data-generated-space-root=""` + `data-generated-space-bootstrap=""`
  markers, drop the SDK bundle) → move every asset under `assets/` → patch runtime paths to
  `./assets/…` → `share share-space --slug <slug>`. Rebuild before publishing (stale-artifact trap).
- **GitHub Pages is opt-in** — only when the user explicitly asks (or needs VM-independent
  hosting, since a `local` Space needs the VM running): `npm run build && npx gh-pages -d dist`.
- The #1 deploy break: the Space daemon serves **only `assets/`**, so any runtime-fetched path
  (PanelUI `config`, gltf/audio/textures) 401s unless it resolves under `./assets/` — and the same
  root-absolute `config: '/ui/…'` 404s on a GitHub subpath. Author configs relative.

### Phase 6 — Iterate → `references/07-iterate.md`

- Re-run the full Phase 4 gate **every** round against _this_ build; never reuse a prior round's
  counts/claims. Recover an unresponsive runtime; re-sync adapters after dependency changes.

## When to load references (route by what you're doing)

| If you're… / you hit…                                                  | Load                                |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| "nothing is ticking / `ecs systems` is empty / dead loop"              | `references/02-world-and-loop.md`   |
| "a panel won't appear or bind"                                         | `references/04-spatial-ui.md`       |
| "401/404 on panels or assets **after deploy**" (Space or Pages)        | `references/06-build-and-deploy.md` |
| "build passed — is it done?"                                           | `references/05-verify-runtime.md`   |
| confirming a real member/signature (instead of casting to `any`)       | `references/api-quick-reference.md` |
| any `iwsdk` command, warmup recovery, or "does this subcommand exist?" | `references/cli-reference.md`       |
| a raw error string you can't place                                     | `references/troubleshooting.md`     |

Run the bundled gates with `bash scripts/preflight-checks.sh` (non-ASCII glyphs, Yoga shorthand,
root-absolute UI configs, CJS idioms, uikitml→json coverage) before deploying.

## Top friction points (most-wasteful mistakes, by phase)

1. Shipping a **no-op game loop** via raw rAF / `world.onUpdate` / `world.ecs.registerSystem` — _P1_.
2. Reinventing **PanelUI readiness** with `setTimeout` instead of the `'qualify'` event — _P3_.
3. **Assets the Space daemon won't serve** (everything fetched at runtime must live under `assets/`) / root-absolute UI config that 404s on a GitHub subpath — _P3/P5_.
4. Treating a green `vite build` as **verification** (it doesn't type-check) — _P4_.
5. Casting `(world as any)` / guessing APIs instead of warming up the **reference CLI** — _all phases_.
6. Building on **Node 18** (Vite 7 `crypto.hash`) or cloning a prior game as a scaffold — _P0_.

item.thinking: Good, I've read the concept image and the IWSDK dev skill. Now let me understand the current state of the code and plan my approach. Let me first check what GLB models are available and read the current source code to understand what needs changing.
Now I have a clear picture of what needs to change. Let me also check the theme system and understand the full scope.
Good. Now I understand the full picture. Let me also check the `applyNeonTint` function and the lights/fog setup, and find where the background color and fog are applied:

Now I have a complete picture. The changes I need to make:

1. **Replace wireframe floor/ceiling with terrain tiles** — remove lines ~935-948 (wireframe floor/ceiling), add terrain tile grid + terrain edges + scattered rocks/craters
2. **Remove floating decorations** — the loop at ~1108-1125 that creates 14 random wireframe shapes, and the ambient particles at ~1127-1139
3. **Remove floating decoration animation** — the code at ~1990-2010 that animates floatSpeed and driftX
4. **Update themes to bright daytime** — the default theme "Neon Holodeck" has bg/fog `#050510` (near-black). Need bright sky colors.
5. **Remove applyNeonTint calls** from environment objects to keep the original Kenney materials
6. **Improve environment layout** — add more structures with better positioning, scaled appropriately to match the rocket
7. **Fix lighting** — increase ambient brightness and strengthen directional light for a proper daytime appearance
8. **Load additional terrain models** — bring in terrain_side, terrain_sideCorner, craterLarge, rocks_smallB, hangar_roundA and other assets

This is substantial work, so I need to approach it methodically. Let me start by identifying the key sections in the code that need modification. I'll tackle the biggest visual changes first—updating the theme colors to bright daytime, cranking up the lighting, and replacing the wireframe floor with solid terrain. Then I'll strip out the floating decorations and their animation, remove the neon tint effect from environment objects, and rebuild the scene layout with more models. After each major change, I'll take a screenshot to make sure it's looking right.

item.text: Now I have a clear picture of everything that needs to change. Let me work through this systematically. First, start the dev server so I can validate as I go:

item.name: edit
item.arguments:

Now I can see the full layout. Several issues:

1. **Terrain tiles are pink/salmon** — Kenney tiles have a sci-fi color that clashes with the sandy ground plane. They create a tiled grid look, not natural terrain.
2. **Too many support structures** — the right side is cluttered with repeated stairs/supports. 
3. **Scales need tuning** — some things too small, others too big.

The concept shows natural rocky terrain, not modular tiles. Let me pivot: use the solid ground plane as base with scattered terrain features (ramps, rocks, craters) for natural-looking landscape rather than a tile grid.

Let me rethink the terrain ramp approach. The Kenney `terrain_rampLarge` model is likely a ramp/slope tile, not a mountain/mesa. It probably looks like a flat triangular platform when viewed from above/side. These aren't the right models for "distant mountains/mesas". 
I should swap these out for better landscape features — using large-scale rock models as distant formations, cliff faces for vertical elements, and dropping the ramps entirely since they're not creating the right visual effect.
